subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Research question is clearly stated, focused and effectively connected to the discussion throughout.
Topic is communicated accurately with clear local context, headings and data reinforcing focus.
Methodology section is appropriately placed and sources are relevant and largely reliable.
weaknesses: Methodology lacks detailed procedures for source selection and data handling, remaining largely descriptive.
Use of first-person narrative in methodology undermines academic objectivity.
Restatement of the research question in the conclusion is too generic and could better reflect specific findings.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission demonstrates excellent focus and a clear, appropriately scoped research question (two strands at top band) but falls short of the highest mark by providing only a mid-band treatment of methodology selection and justification. An overall mark of 5/6 reflects this strong but not flawless performance.

strengths: Source material is clearly relevant and well integrated into the narrative, with data tables and graphs directly mobilised to test policy effectiveness.
Demonstrates a coherent grasp of key economic concepts (externalities, welfare loss, elasticity) with mostly effective use of sources.
Terminology and concepts are accurate and used consistently, with well-labelled diagrams and correct presentation of equations.
weaknesses: Occasional gaps, such as the absence of a formal theoretical definition of negative externalities to scaffold empirical analysis.
Minor phrasing slips do not undermine overall clarity but indicate room for polish.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Knowledge and understanding are consistently strong, with only minor gaps in formal definition and phrasing. The overall performance aligns with the lower end of the top band, meriting 5/6.

strengths: Effective integration of theory and empirical evidence, with welfare diagrams interpreted and PED computed.
Research is broad and clearly relevant, triangulating multiple data sources to address the research question.
Discussion acknowledges alternative explanations and proposes policy extensions, showing evaluative awareness.
weaknesses: Reliance on secondary data limits causal inference; proxy measures (vehicle registrations, noise complaints) lack full contextual discussion.
Some analytical treatments oversimplify (e.g., elasticity formula) and overstate causality without controlling for confounding factors.
Evaluation remains uneven in places, with limitations noted but lacking concrete methodological proposals.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Critical thinking is well developed, placing the work solidly in the 7â9 band. The report achieves clear analysis and reasoned argumentation but is held back from the top band by methodological simplifications and uneven depth of evaluation, warranting 7/12.

strengths: Report follows IB structural conventions (title page, contents, numbered headings, clear sequence).
Visuals and tables are integrated with captions, consistent font and spacing, and required formatting elements (word count, page numbers).
weaknesses: Minor formatting issues include missing axis units in one diagram and an off-centre table.
Residual LaTeX suggestions and occasional heading hierarchy slips detract from full polish.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation largely meets IB requirements and demonstrates clearly appropriate structure, but minor layout and formatting flaws prevent full marks. A mid-band score of 3/4 accurately reflects this performance.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly stated on the title page and restated in the introduction and conclusion.  It isolates two specific policy instruments and one measurable outcome (reduction of ICV externalities), giving a sharp, manageable scope.  Each analytical section returns to this question, and the sub-headings mirror the RQ structure, demonstrating excellent focus and integration.  The question is neither too broad nor too narrow, fully satisfying the highest-band descriptors.

The studentâs topic is announced in the title, framed in the opening paragraph with clear local context and motivation, and revisited consistently throughout the essay.  The focus on two named government measures (FRTC and EHSS) and their connection to negative externalities in Hong Kong is entirely appropriate for an Economics EE and is sustained across all sections.  Headings, diagrams and data tables work together to reinforce the focus.  Minor lapses in wording (e.g. occasional repetition) do not materially impede clarity, so the work sits confidently in the top band.

Government statistics, scholarly studies and news reports are woven into tables, graphs and narrative with clear citation.  Data on EV sales, pollutant concentrations and complaint numbers are directly mobilised to test the effectiveness of the two policies.  Although some figures (e.g., noise-complaint series) are acknowledged as imperfect proxies, the overall deployment of evidence is consistently relevant and well linked to arguments, fulfilling the highest band, albeit with minor gaps that justify the lower end of that band.

The report follows IB EE structural conventions precisely: title page, abstract substitute, contents, numbered headings, introductionâmethodologyâanalysisâconclusion sequence, limitations and bibliography.  Visuals and tables are integrated with captions.  Minor omissions (missing TOC sub-page numbers, occasional heading hierarchy slips) are present but do not detract from a clearly appropriate overall structureâhence the top band with full marks.

The layout meets core IB requirements: word count, title, page numbering, consistent font and spacing, and generally clean tables/figures.  Minor issuesâmissing axis units in one diagram, an off-centre table, and a residual LaTeX suggestionâindicate that not every formatting detail is flawless.  Consequently the work earns a strong but not perfect score within the 3-4 band.

The discussion weighs policy successes against shortcomings and acknowledges alternative explanations (COVID-19 traffic effects, data limitations, possible publication bias).  The student proposes extensions (raising charger standards, streamlining EHSS paperwork) but evaluation depth is uneven; noise-pollution analysis remains tentative and welfare-loss estimates are not quantified.  The argument is reasoned and partially critical, meriting the 7-9 band, lower end.

Analysis threads theory with empirical evidence: welfare diagrams are interpreted, PED computed, and time-series trends linked to policy timing.  Conclusions about diminishing marginal impact after 2022 are logically drawn.  Nonetheless, some numerical treatments (e.g., PED magnitude, welfare-loss measurement) are simplified, and causality occasionally overstated, so the work fits solidly within the 7-9 band rather than the top 10-12 band.

The essay demonstrates a solid, coherent grasp of externalities, elasticity, substitute goods and welfare economics.  Explanations of diagrams are accurate and integrated, and theoretical terminology (MSC, MSB, PED, welfare loss) is handled confidently.  Occasional slips (e.g., âdecrease the welfare loss comparatively to Figure 2â phrasing) do not undermine the clarity of knowledge.  The range and correctness of content align with the top band.

Most data originate from official or peer-reviewed sources and are clearly relevant to the RQ.  The essay triangulates sales data, pollution metrics and complaint statistics to appraise policy impact.  While the reliance on secondary data is a limitation, the breadth and pertinence of research place the work comfortably in the 7-9 band rather than the partially-relevant 4-6 band.

The student relies almost entirely on secondary data drawn from government departments, Statista, SCMP and academic articles, and provides a short justification of source reliability and cross-referencing.  While the sources are largely appropriate, the methodology section remains descriptive and conventional, offering limited explanation of selection criteria or data-handling procedures.  Absence of primary research and scant detail on statistical treatment prevent placement in the top band, but the range and relevance of sources merit a secure mid-high band score.

Economic terminology is used precisely and consistently (e.g., âpositive externality of consumptionâ, âmarginal social benefitâ, âPED elasticâ).  Diagrams are labelled with standard conventions, equations are correctly presented, and narrative integrates terms accurately.  Minor typographical inconsistencies do not detract from overall command, warranting full marks in this strand.

annotations.comment: The use of first-person narrative (âI have seenâ¦â) in the introduction to the methodology section undermines academic objectivity; adopt a formal third-person tone.
The subsection heading â3.3 The positive externality of consumption by Electric carsâ clearly directs the reader to the intended economic concept.
Table 1 uses vehicle registrations as a proxy for demand without discussing limitations of this metric; consider outlining potential biases or alternative measures.
The table of contents omits page numbers for subsections (e.g., 3.1, 3.2), hindering quick navigation and reducing structural clarity.
The title and research question are communicated with precision and clear focus from the outset, providing strong coherence and a well-defined scope aligned with the investigationâs objectives.
Including the word count on the title page meets IB formatting requirements and demonstrates attention to presentation conventions.
The âTable of Contentsâ heading is clearly styled and positioned, establishing a professional and navigable structure.
The impact of FRTC is quantified in HKD but lacks context against average vehicle price; include relative percentages to demonstrate scale of the incentive.
The analysis cogently links reduction in public complaints to decreased negative externalities, demonstrating effective application of source data.
The âMethodologyâ heading is clearly marked and appropriately placed, guiding the reader effectively through the reportâs structure.
The methodology description lacks detailed procedures for source selection and data handling, leaving the approach largely descriptive rather than systematically justified.
The âTheoretical Backgroundâ section is clearly signposted, demonstrating effective organization and coherence with disciplinary expectations.
The discussion of negative externalities would be strengthened by including a formal definition from economic theory to scaffold the empirical analysis.
Figure 1 effectively visualizes the welfare loss triangle and enhances understanding of the theoretical concept of negative externalities.
Figure 7 analysis omits discussion of confounding variables (e.g., industrial emissions, weather) that could influence pollutant concentrations beyond EV adoption.
Equations are presented using `<math>` tags rather than LaTeX; standardize all formulas with `$$â¦$$` for consistent, IB-compliant formatting.
The text misstates that policies âmitigated the positive externality,â introducing conceptual confusion; clarify that policies reduced welfare loss associated with under-consumption.
Axes on Figure 2 lack units (e.g., price in HKD, quantity in units); include explicit units to comply with scientific presentation standards.
Language linking pollutant decreases to EV policies demonstrates sound analytical integration of empirical evidence with theoretical predictions.
Table 2 reports average price in USD thousands, conflicting with earlier HKD values; ensure currency and unit consistency throughout analysis.
The LaTeX block presenting the percentage increase calculation is well-structured and enhances clarity of the computation.
The narrative claims policies âmitigated the positive externality of consumption,â but conflates positive and negative externalities; revise for conceptual accuracy.
Caption labels this as âFigure 6â despite sequential numbering; normalize figure numbering to maintain coherence.
The extension of EHSS and additional funding is supported with concrete figures, illustrating adaptive policy responses to demand evidence.
Phrasing such as âhelped fluctuate the numbersâ is imprecise; replace with clear economic terminology describing the observed correlation.
Figure 8 overlooks potential pandemic lockdown effects on complaint volumes; incorporate discussion of traffic restrictions to contextualize the data trends.
Noise-complaint data aggregates non-vehicle sources; acknowledge this proxyâs limitations or seek more targeted vehicle noise statistics.
The discussion explicitly notes data limitations in noise-complaint figures, reflecting evaluative awareness of methodological constraints.
Figure 5 is labeled as the EV market but illustrates post-intervention ICV market shifts; relabel accurately to avoid reader confusion.
The PED definition is clearly stated in LaTeX, reinforcing conceptual understanding before numerical application.
The restatement of the research question in the conclusion is too generic; refine it to reflect the specific findings and policy nuances uncovered.
Limitation discussion suggests comparative analysis but stops short of proposing a concrete research design; outline specific comparative time-series methods.
The text effectively ties shifts in ICV demand to welfare outcomes, integrating diagrammatic theory with quantitative evidence.
The PED calculation employs simple percentage changes rather than the midpoint (arc elasticity) method; adopt a consistent elasticity formula for accuracy.
The section on non-price determinants thoroughly examines EHSSâs impact on accessibility and social desirability, linking policy to consumer behavior.
The heading â4.3 Non-price determinants of demand for EVs in Hong Kongâ is clearly styled and guides the reader to the topic of analysis.
While expert critiques are cited, quantitative linkage between application quotas and actual uptake would strengthen the evaluative discussion.
The text balances competing expert views effectively and acknowledges differences in stakeholder incentives, enhancing critical depth.
The â4.4 Target set by the governmentâ heading is prominent and maintains logical flow by signposting key evaluative criteria.
The synthesis of pollutant concentration and complaint trends with policy implementation timelines offers nuanced evaluation and depth.
Quantitative evidence demonstrating target exceedance of charging-station quotas strengthens the argument for policy effectiveness.
The bibliography includes diverse sources but lacks uniform citation style; adopt a single referencing convention to enhance professionalism.
The â6. Limitationsâ heading is well-placed and indicates critical reflection on methodological and contextual constraints.
An isolated final entry on its own page disrupts flow; consider adjusting pagination or merging into previous pages to avoid orphan references.
Several bibliography entries contain broken or truncated URLs; verify and correct links to ensure source accessibility.
Duplicate bibliography entries (e.g., items 9 and 21) create redundancy; remove repetitions to streamline reference list.
Copy of item 30 appears as item 31; ensure each reference is unique and accurately numbered.
